schools.summary: The LORD speaking concerning the laws of issues: 'and the LORD spake unto Moses and to Aaron, saying' — the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron, now to give the laws of issues and bodily discharges (the running issue, the natural emissions, the woman's separation, ch. 15, the speaking, God's command, the new section, the to Moses and Aaron, the priests to judge these uncleannesses, the occasion, after the law of leprosy, now the laws of the issues of the body, the defilements of fallen nature, the continuation of the laws of clean and unclean, God himself giving these intimate laws, showing that nothing in the bodily life of his people is outside his holy ordering, the holiness that reaches into the most private things); the LORD speaking to Moses and Aaron (the priests to judge the issues) — the new law (the laws of bodily discharges, the defilements of nature); the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron — the laws of issues introduced (the providence, God giving the laws of the issues, the holiness reaching into the body) — Henry, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And the LORD spake unto Moses and to Aaron, saying
the LORD speaking concerning the laws of issues
God giving the laws of bodily issues and discharges (ch. 15)
the holiness reaching into the most private things of his people

schools.summary: The running issue and the uncleanness of bodily corruption: 'speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When any man hath a running issue out of his flesh, because of his issue he is unclean' — the issue — 'when any man hath a running issue out of his flesh... he is unclean': a man with a running issue (a morbid, diseased discharge from the flesh, perhaps gonorrhea or a like infirmity) unclean because of it (the witnesses noting the significance — the uncleanness of the bodily issue, the diseased discharge defiling, the corruption flowing from the body, the lesson — the figure of the corruption of fallen nature, the issue a token of the corruption that flows from within man, the defilement that proceeds from the flesh, the corruption of our nature, the uncleanness that issues from the fallen heart, 'from within, out of the heart of men, proceed evil thoughts... all these evil things come from within, and defile the man,' Mark 7:21-23, 'out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh,' Matthew 12:34, the lesson of the deep corruption of human nature, the impurity that issues from the fallen flesh, needing cleansing, the figure — the bodily issue a token of the moral corruption of fallen man, the uncleanness flowing from within, the need of the deeper cleansing); the man unclean because of the issue (the discharge defiling, the corruption) — the figure (the corruption of nature, the defilement from within, the lesson, the impurity of fallen flesh); the running issue, he is unclean — the corruption of nature (the providence, the issue a figure of the corruption of fallen man, the uncleanness from within, Mark 7:21-23) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the running issue signify?

sides.position: The diseased discharge defiling, a figure of the corruption of fallen nature - the uncleanness that flows from within man, the impurity proceeding from the fallen flesh and heart (Mark 7:21-23; Matthew 12:34), the deep corruption of human nature needing the deeper cleansing

themes: When any man hath a running issue out of his flesh, because of his issue he is unclean
the running issue and the uncleanness of bodily corruption (the crux of the corruption of nature)
a figure of the corruption of fallen nature, the uncleanness that flows from within (Mark 7:21-23)
the deep corruption of human nature needing the deeper cleansing (Matthew 12:34)

schools.summary: The uncleanness whether the issue runs or is stopped: 'and this shall be his uncleanness in his issue: whether his flesh run with his issue, or his flesh be stopped from his issue, it is his uncleanness' — the uncleanness held whether the issue actively ran or was stopped (obstructed), for the disease was present either way (the running or stopped, the discharge active or obstructed, the it is his uncleanness, the defilement in both states, the figure, the corruption defiling whether it flows openly or is hidden and stopped, the sin that defiles whether it breaks out or is held within, the inward corruption uncleanly even when not flowing); the issue running (the active discharge) — the uncleanness (the open corruption); 'or his flesh be stopped from his issue, it is his uncleanness' (the issue stopped, the obstructed discharge, yet still unclean, the figure, the corruption defiling even when hidden and not flowing, the inward sin, the providence, the uncleanness in both states, the defilement of the corruption) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And this shall be his uncleanness in his issue: whether his flesh run with his issue, or his flesh be stopped from his issue, it is his uncleanness
the uncleanness whether the issue runs or is stopped
the corruption defiling whether it flows openly or is hidden and obstructed
the figure of the sin that defiles whether it breaks out or is held within

schools.summary: The defilement of the bed and seat of the unclean man: 'every bed, whereon he lieth that hath the issue, is unclean: and every thing, whereon he sitteth, shall be unclean' — every bed the man with the issue lay on, and every thing he sat on, becoming unclean (the bed, defiled by his lying, the seat, defiled by his sitting, the figure, the spreading of the uncleanness, the corruption defiling whatever it touches, the contagion of the defilement, sin's spreading taint that defiles all the sinner uses and touches, 1 Corinthians 5:6, 'a little leaven leaveneth the whole lump'); the bed defiled (by the unclean man's lying) — the spreading (the corruption communicated); 'every thing, whereon he sitteth, shall be unclean' (the seat defiled, by his sitting, the figure, the spreading defilement, the corruption that taints all it touches, 1 Corinthians 5:6, the providence, the things defiled, those who touch them unclean, vv.5-12) — JFB, Gill.

themes: Every bed, whereon he lieth that hath the issue, is unclean: and every thing, whereon he sitteth, shall be unclean
the defilement of the bed and seat of the unclean man
the corruption defiling whatever the unclean man touches, the contagion of the defilement
sin's spreading taint that defiles all the sinner uses (1 Corinthians 5:6)

schools.summary: The defilement and washing of one who touches the bed: 'and whosoever toucheth his bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whoever touched the unclean man's bed having to wash his clothes, bathe, and being unclean until evening (the touching the bed, the contact with the defiled thing, the wash and bathe, the cleansing required, the unclean until the even, the temporary defilement, the figure, the defilement of contact with the corruption, the cleansing by washing and water, the danger of association with the unclean, the need of cleansing, Hebrews 10:22); the one touching the bed (the defilement, the washing required) — the contact (the defilement of association); 'bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the washing and bathing, the cleansing of the defilement, the figure, the cleansing by water, the washing of the defiled, Hebrews 10:22, the providence, the defiled cleansed, the contact with corruption defiling) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And whosoever toucheth his bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even
the defilement and washing of one who touches the bed
the defilement of contact with the corruption, the cleansing by washing and water (Hebrews 10:22)
the danger of association with the unclean

schools.summary: The defilement of one who sits where the unclean man sat: 'and he that sitteth on any thing whereon he sat that hath the issue shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whoever sat where the unclean man had sat having to wash, bathe, and being unclean until evening (the sitting where he sat, the contact with the defiled seat, the wash and bathe, the cleansing required, the figure, the defilement of contact with the corruption, the same as touching the bed, vv.5, the danger of sitting where the unclean sat, the association with corruption defiling); the one sitting where the unclean sat (the defilement, vv.5) — the contact (the defilement of association); 'wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the cleansing required, the washing and bathing, the figure, the cleansing of the defiled, vv.5, the providence, the defiled cleansed) — Gill.

themes: And he that sitteth on any thing whereon he sat that hath the issue shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even
the defilement of one who sits where the unclean man sat
the defilement of contact with the corruption, the cleansing by washing and water (vv.5)
the danger of association with the unclean

schools.summary: The defilement of one who touches the unclean man: 'and he that toucheth the flesh of him that hath the issue shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whoever touched the very flesh (body) of the unclean man having to wash, bathe, and being unclean until evening (the touching the flesh, the direct contact with the unclean person, the wash and bathe, the cleansing, the figure, the defilement of direct contact with the corrupt, the closest association defiling, the danger of touching the unclean, yet the contrast with Christ who touched the unclean and was not defiled but cleansed them, Matthew 8:3; Mark 5:25-34); the one touching the unclean man's flesh (the direct contact) — the defilement (the closest association); 'wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the cleansing required, the washing and bathing, the figure, the defilement of direct contact, the cleansing, yet Christ touched the unclean and cleansed them, Matthew 8:3, the providence, the defiled cleansed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And he that toucheth the flesh of him that hath the issue shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even
the defilement of one who touches the unclean man
the defilement of direct contact with the corrupt, the closest association
yet the contrast with Christ who touched the unclean and cleansed them (Matthew 8:3; Mark 5:25-34)

schools.summary: The defilement of the clean by the spittle of the unclean: 'and if he that hath the issue spit upon him that is clean; then he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — if the unclean man spat on a clean person, the clean one defiled, and having to wash, bathe, and being unclean until evening (the spitting, the spittle of the unclean defiling, the wash and bathe, the cleansing required, the figure, the defilement of the corruption communicated even by the spittle, the smallest contact defiling, the contrast with Christ who used spittle to heal, John 9:6; Mark 7:33, his touch and spittle cleansing rather than defiling); the unclean spitting on the clean (the spittle defiling) — the defilement (the corruption communicated); 'then he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the cleansing required, the washing and bathing, the figure, the defilement by the smallest contact, the cleansing, yet Christ's spittle healed, John 9:6, the providence, the defiled cleansed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And if he that hath the issue spit upon him that is clean; then he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even
the defilement of the clean by the spittle of the unclean
the defilement of the corruption communicated even by the spittle, the smallest contact
yet the contrast with Christ whose spittle healed rather than defiled (John 9:6; Mark 7:33)

schools.summary: The defilement of the saddle of the unclean man: 'and what saddle soever he rideth upon that hath the issue shall be unclean' — any saddle (or seat for riding) the unclean man rode upon becoming unclean (the saddle, the riding-seat defiled, the figure, the corruption defiling whatever the unclean man uses, even the saddle, the spreading taint reaching all the sinner's possessions and uses, as the bed and seat, vv.4, the contagion of the defilement, the spreading of the uncleanness to all the man touches); the saddle defiled by the unclean man's riding (the corruption communicated, vv.4) — the spreading (the taint reaching the saddle); 'shall be unclean' (the saddle unclean, the corruption communicated, the figure, the spreading defilement, the corruption tainting all the man uses, the providence, the saddle defiled, those who touch it unclean, vv.10) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And what saddle soever he rideth upon that hath the issue shall be unclean
the defilement of the saddle of the unclean man
the corruption defiling whatever the unclean man uses, the spreading taint (vv.4)
the contagion of the defilement reaching all his possessions

schools.summary: The defilement of those who touch or carry the unclean things: 'and whosoever toucheth any thing that was under him shall be unclean until the even: and he that beareth any of those things shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whoever touched anything that had been under the unclean man (the bed, seat, saddle) unclean until evening, and whoever carried such things having to wash and bathe (the touching, the defilement of contact, the bearing, the carrying, a closer contact, requiring washing, the figure, the defilement of contact with the corrupted things, the cleansing required for the closer contact of bearing, the danger of handling the defiled); the one touching the defiled things (the contact, vv.5) — the defilement (the corruption communicated); 'he that beareth any of those things shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water' (the carrying, the closer contact, requiring washing, the figure, the deeper defilement of bearing the corrupt, the cleansing required, the providence, the defiled cleansed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And whosoever toucheth any thing that was under him shall be unclean until the even: and he that beareth any of those things shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water
the defilement of those who touch or carry the unclean things
the defilement of contact and the deeper defilement of bearing the corrupt
the cleansing required, the danger of handling the defiled

schools.summary: The defilement of those touched by the unwashed unclean man: 'and whomsoever he toucheth that hath the issue, and hath not rinsed his hands in water, he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whomever the unclean man touched without having rinsed his hands defiled, and that person having to wash and bathe (the not rinsed his hands, the unclean man's hands not washed, so his touch defiled, the the rinsing, implying that if the unclean man had rinsed his hands, his touch might not defile, a mitigation, the figure, the defilement of contact with the unclean, the mitigation by washing, the danger of the unclean touch, the cleansing required, the importance of the washing); the unclean man's touch (with unrinsed hands) defiling — the defilement (the corruption communicated by the touch); 'he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the cleansing required, the washing and bathing, the figure, the cleansing of the defiled, the importance of the washing, the providence, the defiled cleansed, the mitigation by the unclean man's rinsing) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And whomsoever he toucheth that hath the issue, and hath not rinsed his hands in water, he shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even
the defilement of those touched by the unwashed unclean man
the defilement of the unclean touch, the mitigation by washing
the cleansing required, the importance of the washing

schools.summary: The earthen vessel broken and the wooden rinsed: 'and the vessel of earth, that he toucheth which hath the issue, shall be broken: and every vessel of wood shall be rinsed in water' — an earthen vessel the unclean man touched to be broken, and a wooden vessel rinsed (the earthen vessel broken, the porous earthen, absorbing the defilement, broken, not cleansed, the wooden vessel rinsed, the harder wood cleansed by rinsing, the figure, the porous earthen vessel, the figure of the frail body, broken when defiled, 2 Corinthians 4:7, the deeper the defilement absorbed, the more it must be destroyed, the harder wood cleansed, the earthen destroyed — as the garment leprosy, Leviticus 11:33); the earthen vessel broken (the porous earthen absorbing the defilement, Leviticus 11:33) — the destruction (the defiled earthen not cleansed but broken); 'every vessel of wood shall be rinsed in water' (the wooden vessel rinsed, the harder wood cleansed, the figure, the harder cleansed, the earthen destroyed, the depth of the defilement, the providence, the vessels broken or rinsed, the defilement guarded against) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the vessel of earth, that he toucheth which hath the issue, shall be broken: and every vessel of wood shall be rinsed in water
the earthen vessel broken and the wooden rinsed
the porous earthen absorbing the defilement destroyed, the harder wood cleansed (Leviticus 11:33)
the figure of the frail body, the depth of the corruption (2 Corinthians 4:7)

schools.summary: The cleansing of the man with the issue by running water: 'and when he that hath an issue is cleansed of his issue; then he shall number to himself seven days for his cleansing, and wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in running water, and shall be clean' — the cleansing — 'he shall number to himself seven days... and bathe his flesh in running water, and shall be clean': when the issue was healed, the man counting seven days for his cleansing, washing his clothes, and bathing in running (living) water, and being clean (the witnesses noting the significance — the cleansing of the healed man, the seven days of separation, the washing, and the bathing in running water, the lesson — the cleansing by running (living) water, not still water but running, living water, the figure of the Spirit and the word, 'out of his belly shall flow rivers of living water... this spake he of the Spirit,' John 7:38-39, the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Ghost, Titus 3:5, the cleansing of the corruption of nature by the living water of the Spirit, the seven days, the complete time of cleansing, the perfect separation before the clean verdict, the figure — the cleansing of the corruption of fallen nature by the washing of the Spirit and the word, the living water of regeneration, John 3:5, 'born of water and of the Spirit'; Ephesians 5:26, 'the washing of water by the word'); the seven days of cleansing (the complete time, the separation before the verdict) — the figure (the full time of cleansing, the providence, the days numbered, the offering to follow, vv.14-15); bathe in running water — the living water of the Spirit (the providence, the cleansing of the corruption by the living water, John 7:38-39) — Wesley, Keil, Gill.

debates.question: What does the cleansing by running water signify?

sides.position: The cleansing of the corruption of fallen nature by the washing of the Spirit and the word - the living (running) water the figure of the Spirit and regeneration (John 7:38-39; Titus 3:5; John 3:5; Ephesians 5:26), the seven days the complete time of cleansing

themes: And when he that hath an issue is cleansed of his issue; then he shall number to himself seven days for his cleansing, and wash his clothes, and bathe his flesh in running water, and shall be clean
the cleansing of the man with the issue by running water (the crux of the cleansing by the Spirit)
the cleansing of the corruption of fallen nature by the washing of the Spirit and the word
the living (running) water the figure of regeneration (John 7:38-39; Titus 3:5; John 3:5)

schools.summary: The offering of two birds on the eighth day: 'and on the eighth day he shall take to him two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, and come before the LORD unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and give them unto the priest' — on the eighth day (after the seven of cleansing) the man bringing two birds to the priest at the tabernacle (the eighth day, the day after the cleansing week, the day of the offering, the two birds, the offering, one for a sin offering, one for a burnt offering, vv.15, the come before the LORD, the man brought to God after the cleansing, the figure, the offering of atonement after the cleansing, the two birds the poor's offering, the atonement and devotion, the cleansed brought before God); the two birds brought (the offering, vv.15) — the offering (the atonement and devotion); 'come before the LORD... and give them unto the priest' (the man brought before the LORD, after the cleansing, the figure, the cleansed brought to God, the offering for atonement, the providence, the offering given, the atonement to follow, vv.15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And on the eighth day he shall take to him two turtledoves, or two young pigeons, and come before the LORD unto the door of the tabernacle of the congregation, and give them unto the priest
the offering of two birds on the eighth day
the offering for atonement after the cleansing, the cleansed brought to God
the two birds the atonement and devotion (vv.15)

schools.summary: The atonement made for the man with the issue: 'and the priest shall offer them, the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD for his issue' — the priest offering the two birds, one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering, and making atonement for the man's issue (the sin offering, the atonement for the uncleanness, the burnt offering, the devotion, the atonement for his issue, the covering of the defilement, the figure, the atonement for the corruption of nature, the cleansing completed by the atonement, the corruption of fallen nature needing not only cleansing but atonement, fulfilled in Christ, Hebrews 9:14); the sin and burnt offerings (the atonement and devotion) — the figure (the atonement for the corruption); 'the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD for his issue' (the atonement for the issue, the covering of the defilement, the figure, the atonement for the corruption of nature, Hebrews 9:14, the providence, the atonement made, the man cleansed) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And the priest shall offer them, the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for him before the LORD for his issue
the atonement made for the man with the issue
the atonement for the corruption of nature, the cleansing completed by the atonement
the figure of the cleansing through Christ (Hebrews 9:14)

schools.summary: The uncleanness of the natural emission: 'and if any man's seed of copulation go out from him, then he shall wash all his flesh in water, and be unclean until the even' — a natural emission (not a disease, but the natural seed) making the man unclean until evening, requiring washing (the natural emission, not the morbid issue, but the natural, the wash all his flesh, the cleansing, the unclean until the even, the temporary defilement, the figure, the uncleanness even of the natural functions of fallen nature, the reminder of the corruption that attends even the lawful, the need of cleansing in all things, the lesser, temporary defilement, not the disease but the natural); the natural emission (the seed, not the morbid issue) — the defilement (the natural uncleanness); 'then he shall wash all his flesh in water, and be unclean until the even' (the washing and temporary uncleanness, the cleansing of the natural defilement, the figure, the uncleanness of fallen nature even in the lawful, the need of cleansing, Psalm 51:5, the providence, the natural defilement cleansed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And if any man's seed of copulation go out from him, then he shall wash all his flesh in water, and be unclean until the even
the uncleanness of the natural emission
the uncleanness even of the natural functions of fallen nature
the reminder of the corruption that attends even the lawful, the need of cleansing (Psalm 51:5)

schools.summary: The washing of the defiled garment: 'and every garment, and every skin, whereon is the seed of copulation, shall be washed with water, and be unclean until the even' — any garment or skin (leather) on which the seed fell to be washed and unclean until evening (the garment and skin, the things defiled, the washed with water, the cleansing, the unclean until the even, the temporary defilement, the figure, the defilement reaching even to the garments, the cleansing required, the corruption of nature touching even our clothes, the need of washing); the garment or skin defiled (the things touched, vv.16) — the defilement (the corruption reaching things); 'shall be washed with water, and be unclean until the even' (the washing and temporary uncleanness, the cleansing, the figure, the defilement reaching the garments, the cleansing required, the providence, the things cleansed) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And every garment, and every skin, whereon is the seed of copulation, shall be washed with water, and be unclean until the even
the washing of the defiled garment
the defilement reaching even to the garments, the cleansing required
the corruption of nature touching even our clothes (vv.16)

schools.summary: The uncleanness of the marriage bed and the bathing of both: 'the woman also with whom man shall lie with seed of copulation, they shall both bathe themselves in water, and be unclean until the even' — after marital relations, both the man and the woman bathing and being unclean until evening (the both bathe, the cleansing of both, the unclean until the even, the temporary defilement, the figure, the uncleanness attending even the lawful marriage bed, the corruption of fallen nature touching even the ordinance God ordained, the marriage honorable and the bed undefiled, Hebrews 13:4, yet the ceremonial uncleanness a reminder of the corruption of nature, the need of cleansing even in the lawful); the marital relations (the lawful, yet ceremonially defiling) — the figure (the corruption of nature touching even the lawful); 'they shall both bathe themselves in water, and be unclean until the even' (the both bathing, the cleansing of both, the figure, the ceremonial uncleanness even in the lawful, the reminder of the corruption of nature, the marriage honorable yet the cleansing a token of the fall, Hebrews 13:4; Psalm 51:5, the providence, both cleansed, the corruption attending even the lawful) — Clarke, Gill, Wesley.

themes: The woman also with whom man shall lie with seed of copulation, they shall both bathe themselves in water, and be unclean until the even
the uncleanness of the marriage bed and the bathing of both
the ceremonial uncleanness attending even the lawful marriage bed (the marriage honorable, Hebrews 13:4)
a reminder of the corruption of nature, the need of cleansing even in the lawful (Psalm 51:5)

schools.summary: The woman's separation in her issue of blood: 'and if a woman have an issue, and her issue in her flesh be blood, she shall be put apart seven days: and whosoever toucheth her shall be unclean until the even' — the separation — 'she shall be put apart seven days': the woman in her natural issue of blood (the monthly course) set apart, unclean seven days, and whoever touched her unclean (the witnesses noting the significance — the natural uncleanness of the woman's issue, the monthly separation, the ceremonial uncleanness of the natural function, the lesson — the figure of the corruption of fallen nature, the issue of blood a token of the corruption that attends even the natural and lawful, the defilement of fallen flesh, 'I was shapen in iniquity; and in sin did my mother conceive me,' Psalm 51:5, the uncleanness reaching the very springs of life and birth, the corruption transmitted in our nature, the gospel turn — the woman with the issue of blood twelve years who touched the hem of Christ's garment and was healed, who by the law would have defiled him but instead was made whole, his power overcoming the uncleanness, 'and the woman was made whole from that hour,' Matthew 9:20-22; Mark 5:25-34, the figure of the unclean made clean by the touch of Christ, the corruption of nature healed by him who is not defiled but cleanses); the seven days' separation (the ceremonial uncleanness of the natural issue) — the figure (the corruption of nature, the defilement of the fallen flesh, Psalm 51:5); 'whosoever toucheth her shall be unclean until the even' (the touch defiling, the spreading uncleanness, the figure, yet the woman who touched Christ was healed, not he defiled, Mark 5:25-34, the providence, the woman separated, the cleansing to follow) — Calvin, Wesley, Keil, Henry, Gill.

debates.question: What does the woman's issue of blood signify?

sides.position: The natural uncleanness of the issue, a figure of the corruption of fallen nature reaching the very springs of life (Psalm 51:5); and the gospel turn, the woman who touched Christ's garment and was healed (Matthew 9:20-22; Mark 5:25-34), the unclean made clean by the touch of him who is not defiled but cleanses

themes: And if a woman have an issue, and her issue in her flesh be blood, she shall be put apart seven days
the woman's separation in her issue of blood (the crux of the corruption of nature)
a figure of the corruption of fallen nature reaching the springs of life (Psalm 51:5)
the woman who touched Christ made whole, the unclean cleansed by him who is not defiled (Matthew 9:20-22; Mark 5:25-34)

schools.summary: The defilement of the bed and seat of the separated woman: 'and every thing that she lieth upon in her separation shall be unclean: every thing also that she sitteth upon shall be unclean' — every bed she lay on and every seat she sat on in her separation becoming unclean, as with the man's issue (vv.4, the bed and seat, defiled by her lying and sitting, the figure, the spreading of the uncleanness, the corruption defiling whatever she touches, the same contagion as the man's issue, sin's spreading taint reaching all the unclean person uses); the bed defiled by her lying (the spreading, vv.4) — the contagion (the corruption communicated); 'every thing also that she sitteth upon shall be unclean' (the seat defiled, by her sitting, the figure, the spreading defilement, the corruption tainting all she uses, the providence, the things defiled, those who touch them unclean, vv.21-23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And every thing that she lieth upon in her separation shall be unclean: every thing also that she sitteth upon shall be unclean

sin's spreading taint reaching all the unclean person uses

schools.summary: The defilement of one who touches the woman's bed: 'and whosoever toucheth her bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whoever touched the separated woman's bed having to wash, bathe, and being unclean until evening, as with the man's issue (vv.5, the touching the bed, the contact with the defiled, the wash and bathe, the cleansing required, the figure, the defilement of contact with the corruption, the cleansing by washing and water, the same as the man's issue, the danger of contact, the need of cleansing); the one touching the bed (the defilement, vv.5) — the contact (the defilement of association); 'wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the cleansing required, the washing and bathing, the figure, the cleansing of the defiled, vv.5, the providence, the defiled cleansed) — Gill.

themes: And whosoever toucheth her bed shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even

the defilement of contact with the corruption, the cleansing by washing and water (vv.5)
the same as the man's issue, the danger of contact

schools.summary: The defilement of one who touches the woman's seat: 'and whosoever toucheth any thing that she sat upon shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whoever touched anything the separated woman had sat on having to wash, bathe, and being unclean until evening (the touching the seat, the contact with the defiled thing, the wash and bathe, the cleansing required, the figure, the defilement of contact with the corruption, the same as the bed, vv.21, the spreading taint reaching all who touch what she used, the cleansing required); the one touching the seat (the defilement, vv.21) — the contact (the defilement of association); 'wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the cleansing required, the washing and bathing, the figure, the cleansing of the defiled, vv.21, the providence, the defiled cleansed) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And whosoever toucheth any thing that she sat upon shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even
the defilement of one who touches the woman's seat
the defilement of contact with the corruption, the same as the bed (vv.21)
the spreading taint reaching all who touch what she used

schools.summary: The defilement by touching the bed or seat: 'and if it be on her bed, or on any thing whereon she sitteth, when he toucheth it, he shall be unclean until the even' — whether the defiling thing was on her bed or on what she sat on, whoever touched it being unclean until evening (the on her bed or seat, the defiled thing in either place, the touching it, the contact defiling, the figure, the defilement of contact with the corruption wherever it is found, the spreading taint, the corruption defiling by the slightest contact, whether on bed or seat); the defiling thing on the bed or seat (the corruption in either place, vv.21-22) — the contact (the defilement wherever found); 'when he toucheth it, he shall be unclean until the even' (the touch defiling, the temporary uncleanness, the figure, the defilement of contact wherever the corruption is, the providence, the one defiled, the cleansing required) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And if it be on her bed, or on any thing whereon she sitteth, when he toucheth it, he shall be unclean until the even
the defilement by touching the bed or seat
the defilement of contact with the corruption wherever it is found (vv.21-22)
the corruption defiling by the slightest contact

schools.summary: The seven-day uncleanness of the man who lies with the separated woman: 'and if any man lie with her at all, and her flowers be upon him, he shall be unclean seven days; and all the bed whereon he lieth shall be unclean' — a man who lay with the woman in her separation unclean seven days (the deeper defilement), and his bed becoming unclean (the lying with her in her separation, the deeper contact, some noting this as a transgression, others a defilement by inadvertence, the seven days unclean, the deeper, longer defilement, as the woman herself, the figure, the deeper defilement of the closest contact with the corruption, the seven days as the woman's own, the warning against contact with the uncleanness, the corruption communicated in its fulness by the closest union); the man defiled by lying with her (the deeper contact, the seven days' uncleanness) — the figure (the deeper defilement of the closest contact, the warning, against the uncleanness, Leviticus 20:18 forbids it as a transgression); 'he shall be unclean seven days; and all the bed whereon he lieth shall be unclean' (the man unclean seven days, his bed defiled, the deeper, spreading defilement, the figure, the corruption communicated in fulness, the deeper taint, the providence, the man defiled as the woman, the corruption spread) — Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

themes: And if any man lie with her at all, and her flowers be upon him, he shall be unclean seven days; and all the bed whereon he lieth shall be unclean
the seven-day uncleanness of the man who lies with the separated woman
the deeper defilement of the closest contact with the corruption
the warning against the uncleanness (Leviticus 20:18)

schools.summary: The woman's prolonged issue of blood: 'and if a woman have an issue of her blood many days out of the time of her separation, or if it run beyond the time of her separation; all the days of the issue of her uncleanness shall be as the days of her separation: she shall be unclean' — the prolonged issue — 'if a woman have an issue of her blood many days... all the days of the issue of her uncleanness shall be as the days of her separation': a woman with an issue of blood beyond her natural time (a chronic, diseased flux) unclean all the days of the issue, as in her separation (the witnesses noting the significance — the prolonged, diseased issue, not the natural monthly course but a chronic flux, a grievous and wearying infirmity, the lesson — the figure of the long-continuing corruption, the chronic uncleanness that does not pass with the natural time, and the gospel turn — this precisely the case of the woman in the Gospels, who 'had an issue of blood twelve years,' who 'had suffered many things of many physicians... and was nothing bettered, but rather grew worse,' and who came behind Christ in the press and touched his garment and was healed, Mark 5:25-29; Matthew 9:20; Luke 8:43-44, the figure of the chronic corruption of nature, the long-incurable infirmity, healed at last by the touch of Christ when all physicians failed, the unclean made whole by him who is not defiled but cleanses); the prolonged, diseased flux (beyond the natural time, the chronic infirmity) — the figure (the long-continuing corruption, the case of the woman of Mark 5:25-29); the woman unclean all the days of the issue (the prolonged defilement, as her separation) — the figure (the chronic corruption, healed by Christ, Luke 8:43-44, the providence, the long uncleanness, the cleansing to come) — Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the woman's prolonged issue of blood signify?

sides.position: The chronic, diseased flux, a figure of the long-continuing corruption of nature; and the gospel fulfilment, the woman of twelve years' issue who touched Christ's garment and was healed when all physicians had failed (Mark 5:25-29; Matthew 9:20; Luke 8:43-44), the chronic corruption healed by the touch of him who cleanses

themes: And if a woman have an issue of her blood many days out of the time of her separation... she shall be unclean
the woman's prolonged issue of blood (the crux of the woman healed by Christ)
a figure of the chronic, long-continuing corruption of nature
the woman of twelve years' issue healed by the touch of Christ when all physicians failed (Mark 5:25-29; Matthew 9:20; Luke 8:43-44)

schools.summary: The defilement of the bed and seat in the prolonged issue: 'every bed whereon she lieth all the days of her issue shall be unto her as the bed of her separation: and whatsoever she sitteth upon shall be unclean, as the uncleanness of her separation' — through all the days of the prolonged issue, her bed and seat as defiling as in her natural separation (the bed as the bed of her separation, the same defilement, the whatsoever she sitteth upon unclean, the seat defiling, the figure, the chronic corruption defiling all she uses, as in the natural separation, the long-continuing uncleanness spreading its taint, the same contagion through all the days of the issue); the bed defiling as in her separation (the chronic corruption, vv.20) — the contagion (the same defilement through the long issue); 'whatsoever she sitteth upon shall be unclean, as the uncleanness of her separation' (the seat defiling, as in the separation, the figure, the chronic corruption tainting all she uses, the providence, the things defiled through all the days, the cleansing to come, vv.28-30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Every bed whereon she lieth all the days of her issue shall be unto her as the bed of her separation: and whatsoever she sitteth upon shall be unclean, as the uncleanness of her separation
the defilement of the bed and seat in the prolonged issue
the chronic corruption defiling all she uses, as in the natural separation (vv.20)
the long-continuing uncleanness spreading its taint through all the days

schools.summary: The defilement of one who touches the things: 'and whosoever toucheth those things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' — whoever touched the things defiled by the woman in her prolonged issue unclean, and having to wash and bathe (the touching those things, the defiled bed and seat, vv.26, the wash and bathe, the cleansing required, the figure, the defilement of contact with the corruption, the same as in her separation, vv.21-23, the cleansing required, the danger of contact with the chronic uncleanness); the one touching the defiled things (the contact, vv.26) — the defilement (the corruption communicated); 'wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even' (the cleansing required, the washing and bathing, the figure, the cleansing of the defiled, vv.21-23, the providence, the defiled cleansed) — Gill.

themes: And whosoever toucheth those things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes, and bathe himself in water, and be unclean until the even
the defilement of one who touches the things
the defilement of contact with the corruption, the same as in her separation (vv.21-23)
the cleansing required, the danger of contact with the chronic uncleanness

schools.summary: The woman's cleansing after the issue: 'but if she be cleansed of her issue, then she shall number to herself seven days, and after that she shall be clean' — when the issue ceased (was healed), the woman counting seven days, and after that being clean, as with the man's issue (vv.13, the cleansed of her issue, the flux healed, the number seven days, the complete time of cleansing, the after that clean, the verdict, the figure, the cleansing after the healing of the corruption, the seven days the perfect time of cleansing, the corruption healed and the cleansing accomplished, as the man's issue); the seven days numbered after the healing (the complete time, vv.13) — the figure (the full time of cleansing); 'and after that she shall be clean' (the woman clean after the seven days, the verdict, the figure, the cleansing accomplished, the corruption healed, the providence, the woman cleansed, the offering to follow, vv.29-30) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: But if she be cleansed of her issue, then she shall number to herself seven days, and after that she shall be clean
the woman's cleansing after the issue
the cleansing after the healing of the corruption, the seven days the perfect time (vv.13)
the corruption healed and the cleansing accomplished

schools.summary: The woman's offering of two birds on the eighth day: 'and on the eighth day she shall take unto her two turtles, or two young pigeons, and bring them unto the priest, to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation' — on the eighth day the woman bringing two birds to the priest, as the man with the issue (vv.14, the eighth day, the day after the cleansing week, the day of the offering, the two birds, one for a sin offering, one for a burnt offering, vv.30, the bring unto the priest, the offering brought to God, the figure, the offering of atonement after the cleansing, the woman brought to God, the same as the man's issue, the cleansed brought before God with the offering); the two birds brought (the offering, vv.14, 30) — the offering (the atonement and devotion); 'bring them unto the priest, to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation' (the woman bringing the offering to the priest, before God, the figure, the cleansed brought to God, the offering for atonement, the providence, the offering brought, the atonement to follow, vv.30) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And on the eighth day she shall take unto her two turtles, or two young pigeons, and bring them unto the priest, to the door of the tabernacle of the congregation
the woman's offering of two birds on the eighth day
the offering for atonement after the cleansing, the same as the man's issue (vv.14, 30)
the cleansed brought before God with the offering

schools.summary: The atonement made for the woman: 'and the priest shall offer the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for her before the LORD for the issue of her uncleanness' — the priest offering the two birds, one for a sin offering and one for a burnt offering, and making atonement for the woman's issue (the sin offering, the atonement for the uncleanness, the burnt offering, the devotion, the atonement for the issue of her uncleanness, the covering of the defilement, the figure, the atonement for the corruption of nature, the cleansing completed by the atonement, the same as the man's issue, the corruption of fallen nature needing atonement, fulfilled in Christ, Hebrews 9:14); the sin and burnt offerings (the atonement and devotion, vv.15) — the figure (the atonement for the corruption); 'the priest shall make an atonement for her before the LORD for the issue of her uncleanness' (the atonement for her issue, the covering of the defilement, the figure, the atonement for the corruption of nature, Hebrews 9:14, the providence, the atonement made, the woman cleansed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the priest shall offer the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering; and the priest shall make an atonement for her before the LORD for the issue of her uncleanness
the atonement made for the woman
the atonement for the corruption of nature, the cleansing completed by the atonement
the figure of the cleansing through Christ (Hebrews 9:14)

schools.summary: The charge to separate Israel from their uncleanness lest they defile the tabernacle: 'thus shall ye separate the children of Israel from their uncleanness; that they die not in their uncleanness, when they defile my tabernacle that is among them' — the separation — 'thus shall ye separate the children of Israel from their uncleanness; that they die not in their uncleanness, when they defile my tabernacle that is among them': the purpose of all these laws to keep Israel separate from their uncleanness, that they not die by defiling God's tabernacle in their midst (the witnesses noting the significance — the holiness that guards God's dwelling, the laws of cleanness given to keep the people from defiling the sanctuary where God dwelt among them, the uncleanness that, if brought into contact with the holy, brought death, the lesson — the holiness of God who dwells among his people, his presence in the tabernacle demanding cleanness, the uncleanness that defiles his dwelling bringing death, 'be ye holy; for I am holy,' Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16, the awful holiness that cannot endure defilement near it, the warning — 'that they die not in their uncleanness,' the deadly danger of bringing uncleanness before the holy God, the death that defilement of the sanctuary incurs, the figure — the people of God, in whom he dwells by his Spirit, called to holiness and to be separate from defilement, 'what agreement hath the temple of God with idols? for ye are the temple of the living God... be ye separate... and touch not the unclean thing,' 2 Corinthians 6:16-17, 'know ye not that ye are the temple of God... if any man defile the temple of God, him shall God destroy; for the temple of God is holy,' 1 Corinthians 3:16-17, the holiness that guards the dwelling of God among and within his people); the deadly danger of the uncleanness (the death incurred by defiling the holy, the warning) — the holiness (the awful holiness of God's presence, the figure, the danger of bringing defilement before God, the need of cleansing); 'when they defile my tabernacle that is among them' (the tabernacle defiled by uncleanness, God's dwelling among them, the holiness it demands, the figure, the temple of God, the holiness that guards his dwelling, 1 Corinthians 3:16-17; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17, the providence, the people kept separate, the sanctuary guarded) — Calvin, Wesley, Clarke, Gill.

debates.question: What does the charge to separate Israel from uncleanness teach?

sides.position: The holiness that keeps the people from defiling the sanctuary where God dwells, the deadly danger of bringing uncleanness before the holy God (Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16) - a figure of the people of God, his temple, called to holiness and separation from defilement (1 Corinthians 3:16-17; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17)

themes: Thus shall ye separate the children of Israel from their uncleanness; that they die not in their uncleanness, when they defile my tabernacle that is among them
the charge to separate Israel from their uncleanness lest they defile the tabernacle (the crux of the holiness that guards God's dwelling)
the holiness of God who dwells among his people, the deadly danger of defilement (Leviticus 11:44; 1 Peter 1:16)
a figure of the people of God, his temple, called to holiness and separation (1 Corinthians 3:16-17; 2 Corinthians 6:16-17)

schools.summary: The summary of the law of issues begun: 'this is the law of him that hath an issue, and of him whose seed goeth from him, and is defiled therewith' — this beginning the summary of the whole law of issues: the man with the running issue (vv.2) and the man with the natural emission (vv.16, the him that hath an issue, the running issue, the diseased flux, the him whose seed goeth from him, the natural emission, the figure, the gathering of the laws of the issues, the corruption of nature in its various forms, the comprehensive law of the defilements of the body, every kind provided for); the law of the running issue (vv.2) — the summary (the law of the diseased flux); 'him whose seed goeth from him, and is defiled therewith' (the natural emission, vv.16, the figure, the corruption of nature in the natural function, the providence, the whole law of issues gathered, vv.33) — Gill.

themes: This is the law of him that hath an issue, and of him whose seed goeth from him, and is defiled therewith
the summary of the law of issues begun
the gathering of the laws of the issues, the corruption of nature in its various forms (vv.2, 16)
the comprehensive law of the defilements of the body, every kind provided for

schools.summary: The summary of the law of issues for man and woman: 'and of her that is sick of her flowers, and of him that hath an issue, of the man, and of the woman, and of him that lieth with her that is unclean' — the summary including the woman in her separation (vv.19), the man and woman with issues (vv.2, 25), and the man who lay with the unclean woman (vv.24, the her that is sick of her flowers, the woman in her separation, the the man and the woman, both subject to the issues, the him that lieth with her that is unclean, the deeper defilement, vv.24, the figure, the whole law of issues gathered for man and woman, the corruption of nature in all, the comprehensive law of the defilements of fallen flesh, closing the laws of cleanness before the great Day of Atonement, ch. 16); the woman in her separation and the man with the issue (vv.19, 2) — the summary (the law of the issues for both); 'of the man, and of the woman, and of him that lieth with her that is unclean' (the man, the woman, and the one defiled by contact, vv.24, the figure, the corruption of nature in all, the comprehensive law, the providence, the law of issues complete, before the Day of Atonement, ch. 16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And of her that is sick of her flowers, and of him that hath an issue, of the man, and of the woman, and of him that lieth with her that is unclean
the summary of the law of issues for man and woman
the whole law of issues gathered, the corruption of nature in all (vv.2, 19, 24, 25)
closing the laws of cleanness before the great Day of Atonement (ch. 16)
